01 The React Mental Model — UI as a Function of State

← Back to lessons

You've used React. Now understand it. This lesson establishes the core mental model that every other lesson builds on. React isn't a template engine. It's a rendering pipeline that transforms state into UI.

The Core Equation: UI = f(state)

Every React application, from a todo list to Facebook's news feed, is built on one idea:

UI = f(state)

Your entire user interface is the output of a function. The input is state. Change the state, call the function again, get a new UI. That's it. That's React.

This sounds simple, but the implications are enormous. Let's unpack them using an analogy you'll appreciate.

The newspaper analogy. Think of React like a newspaper's printing process. You don't modify yesterday's paper by crossing out words and writing new ones in the margins. You write a new edition. The entire front page is regenerated from the current state of the world. React works the same way — it doesn't patch the old UI. It describes the entire new UI from scratch, then figures out what changed.

Declarative vs Imperative: Why the Distinction Matters

You've heard this before. Let's make it concrete.

Imperative (jQuery / vanilla JS)
// Step-by-step instructions:
const btn = document.getElementById('btn');
const count = document.getElementById('count');
let n = 0;

btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
  n++;
  count.textContent = n;
  if (n > 10) {
    count.style.color = 'red';
  }
  if (n === 1) {
    const badge = document.createElement('span');
    badge.textContent = ' click!';
    count.appendChild(badge);
  }
});
Declarative (React)
// Describe the result:
function Counter() {
  const [n, setN] = useState(0);

  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setN(n + 1)}>
        Click
      </button>
      <span style={{ color: n > 10 ? 'red' : 'white' }}>
        {n}
        {n === 1 && <span> click!</span>}
      </span>
    </div>
  );
}

The imperative version tells the browser how to update: find this element, change this text, add this class. The declarative version says what the UI should look like for any given state, and React figures out the DOM mutations needed to get there.

Why this matters beyond philosophy: Declarative code is easier to reason about because each render is independent. You don't need to trace a chain of mutations to understand the current UI. Given state X, the UI is always Y. This is the same reason pure functions are easier to test than stateful procedures — same input, same output, every time.

The cost of declarative

Nothing is free. The declarative model means React has to:

  1. Call your component function on every render — it re-runs the entire function body, not just the "changed parts"
  2. Diff the output against the previous output to find what actually changed (reconciliation)
  3. Batch the DOM mutations to minimize layout thrashing

Steps 2 and 3 are where React earns its keep. Step 1 is where performance problems usually live — because your function body might be doing expensive work on every render. We'll cover this in Lesson 08.

Components Are Functions (Really)

A React component is literally a function that takes props and returns JSX. This isn't a metaphor. Let's trace what happens:

// This is just a function.
function Article({ title, author, date }: ArticleProps) {
  const formatted = new Date(date).toLocaleDateString();

  return (
    <article>
      <h2>{title}</h2>
      <p>By {author} · {formatted}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

When React renders this component, it literally calls Article({ title: "...", author: "...", date: "..." }). The return value is a JavaScript object — not HTML, not DOM nodes. It's a description of what the UI should look like. React calls this a "React element."

// What Article() actually returns (simplified):
{
  type: 'article',
  props: {
    children: [
      { type: 'h2', props: { children: 'Breaking: Budget Bill...' } },
      { type: 'p', props: { children: 'By Trevor Brown · Mar 18, 2026' } }
    ]
  }
}

JSX is syntactic sugar for function calls. When you write <Article title="..." />, Babel/SWC transforms it into React.createElement(Article, { title: "..." }) (or the modern jsx() runtime). The angle brackets are just a nicer way to write nested function calls. Understanding this demystifies everything about React — conditional rendering, lists, composition. They're all just JavaScript expressions inside a function.

The Render Cycle: What Actually Happens

When you call setState, React doesn't immediately update the DOM. Here's the actual sequence:

  1. Trigger: Something causes a re-render (setState, context change, parent re-render)
  2. Render Phase: React calls your component function to get new JSX. This is pure computation — no side effects, no DOM access.
  3. Reconciliation: React diffs the new element tree against the previous one. It builds a list of changes needed.
  4. Commit Phase: React applies the minimal set of DOM mutations. This is where the actual browser DOM gets updated.
  5. Effects: After the DOM is painted, useEffect callbacks run.

The render phase is not the same as rendering to the screen. This trips people up constantly. "Rendering" in React means calling your function. The DOM update happens later, in the commit phase. Your component can "render" (be called) multiple times and React might throw away the result if something higher-priority comes in. This is the foundation of concurrent features.

A concrete example

function NewsFilter({ articles }: Props) {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [sort, setSort] = useState<'date' | 'relevance'>('date');

  // This runs on EVERY render — but that's OK (usually)
  const filtered = articles
    .filter(a => a.title.toLowerCase().includes(query.toLowerCase()))
    .sort((a, b) => sort === 'date'
      ? b.date.getTime() - a.date.getTime()
      : b.score - a.score
    );

  return (
    <div>
      <input value={query} onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)} />
      <select value={sort} onChange={e => setSort(e.target.value)}>
        <option value="date">Date</option>
        <option value="relevance">Relevance</option>
      </select>
      {filtered.map(a => <ArticleCard key={a.id} article={a} />)}
    </div>
  );
}

Every time the user types a character, React calls this entire function. The filtered array is recomputed from scratch. Is that wasteful? Usually not — JavaScript is fast. Filtering 100 articles takes microseconds. You'd need thousands of items with complex computation before this becomes a problem.

Don't optimize for problems you don't have. The React model encourages you to write straightforward code and only optimize when you measure a real issue. This is the opposite of premature optimization — it's engineering discipline. Like a good editor: don't cut the story until you know what's slow. Profile first, optimize second.

Composition: The Power of Functions Calling Functions

Since components are functions, they compose exactly like functions do. This is React's killer feature — not the virtual DOM, not hooks. Composition.

// Small, focused components
function Avatar({ src, name }: AvatarProps) {
  return <img src={src} alt={name} className="avatar" />;
}

function Byline({ author, date }: BylineProps) {
  return (
    <div className="byline">
      <Avatar src={author.photo} name={author.name} />
      <span>{author.name}</span>
      <time>{date}</time>
    </div>
  );
}

function ArticleCard({ article }: CardProps) {
  return (
    <article>
      <h3>{article.title}</h3>
      <Byline author={article.author} date={article.date} />
      <p>{article.excerpt}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

// Compose them into a page
function HomePage({ articles }: PageProps) {
  return (
    <main>
      {articles.map(a => <ArticleCard key={a.id} article={a} />)}
    </main>
  );
}

Each component is a self-contained unit that knows how to render itself given its props. The parent doesn't know or care about the implementation details of its children. This is the same principle as modular journalism: the headline desk doesn't need to know how the infographics team builds their charts. They just need to know what data to pass down.

The children pattern

The most powerful composition pattern is children. It lets you create wrapper components that don't need to know what they're wrapping:

function Card({ children, variant = 'default' }: CardProps) {
  return (
    <div className={`card card--${variant}`}>
      {children}
    </div>
  );
}

// Use it to wrap anything:
<Card variant="featured">
  <ArticleCard article={leadStory} />
</Card>

<Card variant="sidebar">
  <WeatherWidget />
</Card>

Composition over configuration. When you're tempted to add a prop like showAvatar, showDate, showExcerpt to a component, stop. That's configuration, not composition. Instead, create smaller components that the consumer assembles. This keeps each piece simple and testable.

Immutability: Why React Needs New References

React detects state changes by comparing references, not deep-comparing values. This is why you can't mutate state directly:

Broken: Mutating state
// React won't re-render!
const [articles, setArticles] = useState([]);

function addArticle(article) {
  articles.push(article); // mutation
  setArticles(articles);  // same ref
}
Correct: New reference
// New array = new reference = re-render
const [articles, setArticles] = useState([]);

function addArticle(article) {
  setArticles(prev => [
    ...prev,
    article  // spread into new array
  ]);
}

When you call setArticles(articles) with the same array reference, React's comparison says "same object, nothing changed" and skips the re-render. Even though you pushed a new item into it. React checks Object.is(prevState, newState) — a reference equality check, not a deep comparison.

Why reference equality? Deep comparison is expensive — O(n) for arrays, recursive for nested objects. Reference equality is O(1). React made a deliberate tradeoff: require immutable updates, but make change detection blazing fast. This is why the entire ecosystem uses immutable patterns, spread operators, and libraries like Immer.

Unidirectional Data Flow

Data in React flows one direction: parent to child via props. If a child needs to communicate upward, the parent passes a callback function as a prop.

// Parent owns the state
function SearchPage() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [results, setResults] = useState([]);

  return (
    <div>
      <SearchBar value={query} onChange={setQuery} />
      <ResultsList results={results} />
    </div>
  );
}

// Child communicates up via callback
function SearchBar({ value, onChange }: SearchBarProps) {
  return (
    <input
      value={value}
      onChange={e => onChange(e.target.value)}
      placeholder="Search articles..."
    />
  );
}

This is constraining by design. You always know where state lives and how it changes. In a newsroom analogy: the editor (parent) decides what goes on the page. Reporters (children) can pitch stories (call callbacks), but the editor makes the final decision (updates state).

When unidirectional flow breaks down: When many unrelated components need the same state, passing props through intermediate components ("prop drilling") becomes painful. That's when you reach for Context (lesson 04) or external state managers. But start with props — most "prop drilling" complaints come from not composing components well enough.

Check Your Understanding

1. What does "UI = f(state)" mean in practice?

Your component function is called with the current state, and it returns JSX describing the complete UI for that state. React then diffs this against the previous output and applies the minimum DOM changes. The UI is always a deterministic function of the current state.

2. What's the difference between the "render phase" and the "commit phase"?

The render phase is when React calls your component functions to get new JSX — it's pure computation with no side effects. The commit phase is when React applies the actual DOM mutations. The render phase can be interrupted (concurrent mode); the commit phase cannot.

3. Why does array.push(item); setState(array) not trigger a re-render?

Because push mutates the existing array, so the reference doesn't change. React uses Object.is() to compare the old and new state. Same reference = no change detected = no re-render. You need to create a new array: setState([...array, item]).

4. A component renders 500 items in a list. Should you immediately wrap it in React.memo?

No. First measure whether it's actually slow. Rendering 500 simple list items is typically fast (< 16ms). React.memo adds comparison overhead on every render. Only memoize after profiling shows the component is a bottleneck.

5. Why does React use unidirectional data flow instead of two-way binding?

Predictability. With unidirectional flow, you always know where state lives and how it gets updated. Two-way binding (like Angular's ngModel) can create invisible update cycles where changing one thing triggers changes elsewhere. React makes data flow explicit at the cost of more boilerplate.

Exercise: Trace the Render

Add a console.log('Rendering ArticleList') at the top of several component functions in one of your projects. Then interact with the UI. Answer these questions:

  1. Which components re-render when you type in a search field? Are any of them unnecessary?
  2. How many times does the parent re-render vs the child when you click a button in the child?
  3. If you move state down to the component that actually uses it, do fewer components re-render?

This exercise builds intuition about React's rendering behavior. You'll use this intuition in every lesson that follows.

← Lesson 00: Syllabus Lesson 02: Fiber & Reconciliation →
